case_name: John Doe #533 v. New Hampshire Department of Health and Human Services & a.
transcript_text:
This is case 2025-0092, John Doe number 533 versus New Hampshire Department of Health and Human Services at all.

Attorney Vincenzo, I understand you reserved two minutes for rebuttal.

Correct, Your Honor. Thank you.

May it please the Court. David Vincenzo for John Doe 533.

Your Honors, this case requires the Court to review the Superior Court's application of the discovery rule.

The Superior Court made improper, subjective assumptions about certain facts and resolved conflicting inferences against John Doe 533.

In doing so, it violated this Court's rule most recently articulated in a child abuse case in Troy versus Bishop Gerton, which is just positive here.

The language of the discovery rule is familiar to us all and it's briefed very adequately, so I won't repeat it.

But the standard enunciated in Troy or repeated in Troy is that all evidence and inferences drawn therefrom must be viewed in the light most favorable to the non-moving party.

Here, that's John Doe, the 13-year-old boy who was abused.

Here, as in Troy, the lower court misapplied that standard.

The Court made factual assumptions, not supported by the record evidence.

Of course, it's on a motion to dismiss, so there's hardly any record evidence.

The Court also construed disputed inferences against the plaintiff rather than in the plaintiff's favor.

And ignored other reasonable inferences favoring the plaintiff.

In fact, the Court ignored an awful lot of what the plaintiff said in his written declaration.

I'd really ask the Court to review it if you haven't done it already.

It said appendix one, pages 158 to 168.

There's so much there that the lower court just seemed not to pick up on.

All of this violated the rule on motion to dismiss and made all the difference.

The Court's review here is de novo.

We asked the Court to reverse and to send it back to discovery and ultimately summary judgment or a trial.

I want to go directly to what I consider the biggest error that the Court made,

because it's the most important, it appears to be really the linchpin on which the Court decided the case.

The Court hypothesized that Mount Prospect Academy was a small single group home and had that mental image,

which in its view, given its intimate character meant that the management of Mount Prospect

was physically adjacent or mingled with a resonance and therefore would know what was going on.

None of this is true.

Again, we don't have much of a record, but there is nothing in the record that suggests that MPA is a small intimate little facility.

It's irrelevant with respect to statute limitations, isn't it?

No, it's not, Your Honor, because it's the linchpin on which the Court made the decision on the discovery rule.

The Court theorized that because it's a small little intimate home.

Therefore, the management would be intermingled with the events, would know that the violence was going on,

and if it knew that it was going on and didn't stop it, it must be condoning it.

Then it drew another inference on top of that inference, which was based on a false assumption.

And that is that this 13-year-old boy should have seen all that and noticed that management is nearby,

they don't seem to be stopping it, therefore they must have known.

And therefore, he doesn't get the benefit of the discovery rule.

That's the basis of this decision.

That's in opposite of Troy.

Sorry, Your Honor?

That's in opposite of our holding in Troy, isn't it?

I think it is, Your Honor.

Exactly.

I think Troy controls this case.

I don't think the Court has a very difficult job here.

Regrettably, the lower Court, for whatever reason, somehow I had the idea that this is a small intimate home.

Mount Prospect Academy is actually bigger than the Sumeen Center.

It's more sprawling.

It has 10 locations.

And there's nothing in the record suggesting that it's a small little intimate place.

I'm missing the connection between the size of the facility and how your client knew or should have known what happened was...

I agree with you, Your Honor.

There really shouldn't be a connection because the Court assumed that it's a small little place.

If it's a small little place, management should have known that violence was happening, that abuse was happening.

And if it didn't stop it, it condoned it.

Then from that, there's yet another inference that this 13-year-old boy should have seen all that.

Should have seen that management doesn't seem to be...

They know about it.

They must know about it because it's a small little intimate place.

They're not doing anything about it.

Therefore, me as a 13-year-old boy should have known that management's in on the deal, that they're complicit.

That was the lower Court's reasoning.

I mean, I just stating it sort of makes the point.

There's first a false assumption, then an inference, then another inference, and then another inference.

Is that part of your argument that your client sort of developed this mistrust of adults in his life

and that that is the linchpin to the analysis?

That doesn't have to be the linchpin, Judge.

It is part of our factual scenario.

We think that the Court's standards should be broad enough to consider that.

This Court has a long history of issuing decisions on the discovery rules suggesting that all the circumstances,

all the factual circumstances have to be considered in determining whether or not the plaintiff's decision-making

on the issue of the discovery rule was reasonable.

Now, the state is arguing now for something that's very different than what the case law in this state is.

They're looking for a very narrow, simplistic definition of knowledge.

They call it knowledge of facts.

I don't know what that means.

First, they don't provide a definition of what knowledge means.

And really, we're at the motion to dismiss days.

There are no facts.

They're just assertions.

And then maybe a speculation on what might later be filled in if there's discovery.

Part of your argument is that he made reports to therapists.

Is that right?

No, Your Honor.

I don't think so, Your Honor.

I mean, that is their, I mean, let me go back to, and I think I'll answer it this way,

that the state is suggesting that this narrow view of knowledge, that if you knew fact one and knew fact two,

that you automatically know how they work together and what they mean.

If you go to any dictionary, let me go first to the legislature.

The legislature didn't say mere knowledge.

The legislature says reasonable knowledge.

Now what is reason?

Reason is a process.

Reason is not a static thing.

It is a verb.

Two reason.

And the process of reasoning is what we do when we perceive events, what the state calls facts,

and they're not facts.

They're just perception of events.

And we actually apply reason to them, and then we draw reasonable conclusions from them.

That is what the statute envisions.

And that's also supported by at least 50 years of case law, starting with Raymond versus Lily,

which says that a reason person has to consider all the facts, all the circumstances,

not just some sort of very narrow little definition.

And then Troy, of course.

Troy itself considered, in that case, the young woman had not brought her suit for 23 years after the events that occurred.

And the court found that on summary judgment, even after a developed record,

the court had aired by construing inferences against the plaintiff in that case

and depriving her of a Part I Article 14 right to a remedy.

I want to make sure I understand your view.

Is it your view, the plaintiff's discovery of facts necessary that it depends on whether the plaintiff is unthinkable

and is, for example, in this case, of the role of not pleasant academy?

Well, there can be a duty of inquiry.

Yes, if there's a sufficient amount of information or evidence or reasonable knowledge to trigger it.

Is it dependent on inquiry notice?

The reason I ask is I look at the statute and the statute.

Yes, and we've also submitted with our presentation again.

It's not very developed because we're on a motion to dismiss.

But the reports of two national experts on the issue of childhood trauma.

It's different when it's a kid.

It's different when you're 13 years old and you have no advantages in life.

And you've already been abused.

You've already been traumatized.

You have parents that are incompetent.

That's why you end up at a place like MPA or YDC.

It's unfortunate.

But to not take into account the situation of the actual plaintiff,

what the court in Willette, which was cited in Troy with great approval,

the Willette case is the first circuit case.

And it says that the reasonable person has to be similarly situated to the specific plaintiff.

And because what else could this reasonable person be?

A 46-year-old judge?

No.

Where are we going to draw the line?

It's got to be the person who actually has the constitutional right to a remedy.

It's got to be that person.

Now, it's still an objective standard.

And the subjective view of the plaintiff is not dispositive.

But that doesn't mean it's irrelevant.

Just as in the criminal law, the perception of the victim is very, very important evidence.

And here the perception of the victim was this.

He got conflicting stories from the Mount Pleasant staff.

And what it does is reflect what you, Judge Donovan, noted in Troy.

That in child abuse cases, we find ourselves, they arise from various

and complicated factual settings.

Very complicated.

How do you respond to the trial court's order that states,

plaintiff does not allege any fact that he learned about Mount Prospect

since leaving its facility in 2008.

He says only that he learned from fellow inmates in June of 22

that there was a lawsuit pending.

And he only learned about the institutional responsibility of Mount Prospect

after talking with a pastor sometime thereafter.

What's your response?

Well, when he's 13 years old, he's a 13-year-old, I remember it well.

It's a good thing I had good parents because otherwise I would have been at YDC probably.

Seriously, I'm 13-year-old's unformed brain.

Got at least another 12 years before that brain fully forms.

And he is told by the Mount Prospect staff that what's happening to you is normal.

It's discipline.

It's what you deserve because you're a bad kid.

And he internalized that and he came to believe.

This is just what happens to kids who are bad.

You know, I'm a bad kid.

And he held that belief as often as the case.

You get an impression, a belief at 13.

Sometimes people go their whole life and never change that belief.

And, you know, the legislature here is found in other contexts and the statute of limitations

that it's different when it happens to a kid.

It's different.

That's why we have continually lengthened the statute of limitation for child abuse

in the state because the legislature recognized that when the abuse happens when you're a kid,

it has a different effect on you.

You get an impression at 13.

You may retain it till you die at 70 or 80, you may.

In this case, he didn't.

He didn't report it to the police.

He ran away, judge.

And again, this is part of the undeveloped record.

I mean, you have a little snippet that says, I ran away and I said to the police,

you know, bad things are happening to me there.

Well, he's a 13-year-old boy.

I'm sure that if he, you know, if he was getting spanked or if he was otherwise being deprived

of things to make him discipline, legitimate discipline, discipline that was privileged,

he wouldn't like that either.

You dispute that he...

Well, I do.

Yes, I do, Your Honor.

I think that's a very ambivalent concept.

He just tells the police, I don't like what's going on there.

You know, they locked me up.

They...

I don't know.

It's not very clear what he does say.

It's an undeveloped record.

We're on a motion to dismiss.

He hasn't had a full opportunity to develop it.

But the police are like, yeah, yeah, yeah, the kid doesn't like being locked.

Well, you should have obeyed your mother.

You should have gone to school, whatever.

You know, you could see how easily that would be dismissed and how easily it could...

So whether it's dismissed or not, if he's making that report,

doesn't that show knowledge of how he felt about it?

Yes, it shows that he didn't like it, that he was uncomfortable.

That doesn't mean it's not permissible.

It doesn't at all mean that...

I mean, this is a congregate facility for kids who are already acting out.

The use of physical discipline, use of restraints,

the use of occasionally solitary confinement was routine.

And viewed as legitimate.

I mean, it probably shouldn't have been.

Excuse me, Judge.

That's right, your light is on.

Oh, I'm sorry, Your Honor.

Thank you.

Thank you, Your Honor.

Attorney D. Pesquale?

Good morning, Your Honor.

Good morning.

May it please the court.

Mr. Frank D. Pesquale from Mount Prospect Academy.

I would agree with my brother, counsel,

in the sense that this is not a complicated case.

And the order from the Superior Court should be affirmed.

There is no dispute that this lawsuit was filed

after the statute of limitations expired.

In fact, more than eight and a half years

after the statute of limitations expired.

So what's the difference between this case and Troy?

Lots.

Well, Troy took the existing case law

and applied it to the facts in Troy.

The case law remains the same here.

And in fact, what Troy did was just apply the facts to that case.

That's what needs to be done here.

The facts in Troy were very different.

You had a school that reportedly hired a known sex offender

contrary to law.

You had two isolated incidents of sexual abuse

against the plaintiff in that case.

One person, two incidents.

The plaintiff in that case reported it to the school

who knew or should have known, as I read Troy,

that they hired a sex offender

and now he was getting a report from the woman

than a student, that she's being sexually assaulted.

And the administration said,

you tell anybody about this

and you're going to get in trouble.

Those are very different facts,

but the most important fact, and you brought it up

just as done of him before,

there's a new fact in Troy.

The new fact, and as I read Troy,

it was undisputed that 21 years later,

the plaintiff in Troy learned that the school admitted

and it hired a sex offender.

And that's important because that's a new fact.

And that new fact led to specific claims against the school,

negligent hiring the sex offender

and negligent security of me, the student.

We don't have that here.

This argument about the size of Mount Prospect Academy,

I respectfully submit a subterfuge.

What is important is that the plaintiff here,

when the burden passed, and that's another argument

that I respectfully submit a subterfuge,

when the statute of limitation is raised

and shown by a defendant as an affirmative defense,

whether it's the motion to dismiss phase

or the summary judgment phase,

the burden passes to the plaintiff,

here the appellant, and it's the appellant's burden

to prove the applicability of the discovery rule

or some other equitable tolling.

We've heard there's no record.

Well, it was incumbent on the plaintiff,

the appellant here, to create that record.

And in fact, what's happened down

in Rockingham Superior Court,

you have upwards of 1,600 of these cases.

And the process that was developed

to respond to these hundreds of cases

was a very reasoned one.

How are we going to deal with the discovery rule?

The appellant had the ability to raise the record,

and he did.

He submitted a declaration.

The declaration belies any claim

that the discovery rule applies.

The declaration establishes that the appellant knew

he was injured during each of these instances

that's alleged during his time at Mount Prospect

between 2006 and 2008.

He knew he was injured.

He knew who had done it.

And that, respectfully, forecloses any application

of the discovery rule.

The argument here is that, well, he didn't know.

He did know.

He submitted a declaration in which he says

that this abuse was so commonplace

it happened all the time.

And the declaration that applies

to Mount Prospect Academy,

it's not the whole declaration

because the co-defendant state

will indicate, but really paragraphs,

forgive me for looking at my notes,

paragraphs 14 to 22 of the declaration.

It's in the appendix about page 160 to 162, Appendix 1.

In there, he specifies that the abuse was so pervasive,

it was almost every day,

whether it was being put in solitary confinement.

Almost all the time, the appellant says,

he was subjected to painful and violent restraints.

He also claims that on multiple occasions,

at least 12, he was subjected to violent physical abuse.

And he says that he and others at Mount Prospect Academy

were goaded into fighting each other

so that way, ostensibly, there would be an excuse

to use more restraints on him and his others.

What the appellant says is,

I was injured during these restraints

and during this physical abuse,

injuring my head, my neck, my back,

and primarily my shoulder, which still bothers me to this day.

He alleges that he knows he was injured.

He alleges he knows who hurt him.

And the pervasiveness, as this Rockingham Superior pointed out,

post-Troy, analyzing this case,

found that the pervasiveness shows that he knew

or should have known that this was a commonplace,

so commonplace that Mount Prospect had to have fostered this abuse.

And if you look at the case law from Glyne's to Perez,

you don't have to be certain of the causal connection,

but is there a possibility of the causal connection?

And here, the abuse is so pervasive,

not one as in Troy person, but multiple staff members

at one group home acting in concert to hurt him and others.

That's what he says in his declaration,

out in the open.

This is so pervasive that it would, at the very least,

with the duty to inquiry, require the plaintiff

to know that there's a possibility that Mount Prospect

is fostering this, allowing it to happen.

The plaintiff's own declaration belies the discovery.

And I would leave it at that, but the light's on, if I could.

And just by saying...

You have five minutes.

Well, thank you.

There is no indication of any sort of mental disability

or mental health disability by John Doe, 533.

And so this argument that there should be a different standard

is really, by the appellant, arguing for a subjective standard,

which does not apply.

It's an objective standard from the reasonable person,

not subjective, what did you think?

And that's important because here,

the own words from the appellant establishes

he knows what happened.

He knew he was injured when it happened through 2008.

He knows who did it.

He should have known, at the very least,

there was the possibility that the causation

led back to Mount Prospect given the pervasiveness.

And as your Honor had pointed out,

he points to nothing new since 2008

that he learned, unlike Troy.

There's no discovery rule here.

Nor is there any reason to somehow say

that he should be looked at differently.

And not only is there no legal, I think,

proposition or support for that,

there's also no factual support.

My brother, counsel, refers to these expert reports.

If you look at those expert reports,

they don't say anything about John Doe, 533.

They didn't look at his records.

They didn't talk to him.

They didn't examine him.

They don't even reference him.

This is just sort of boilerplate, well, it could be.

That's not the wall because it's not subjective

when we look at the discovery rule.

And it's not the facts of this case.

It hasn't been raised.

So with that in mind, this is not a complicated case.

He knew he was injured.

He knew when it happened.

He knew where it happened, who did it,

and at the very least, with the duty of inquiry,

he should have inquired further to know

it would, based on his own allegations,

we have to take his word that it was Mount Prospect.

And for those reasons, the lower court

order of dismissal should be affirmed.

I'm happy to answer any other questions.

Thank you, counsel.

Thank you.

May it please the court?

My name is Mary Trick and I'm here on behalf of the Department

of Health and Human Services.

Since I only have five minutes,

I'll take questions at any time

and I'm going to try not to repeat

what others have already said.

I do want to go back to Justice School

with your question about inquiry notice.

And I do think that in most cases,

inquiry notice is going to be a fact question,

which you'll need to proceed probably

to summary judgment or further on.

But here we have facts alleged in the declaration

that don't require us to go to inquiry notice, right?

We have not only the pervasiveness of the assault,

but we have the plaintiff using the phrase

apparently empowered, right?

He observed the staff in the facility

and the abuse in the facility to be so pervasive

that the staff was apparently empowered

by Mount Prospect to engage in this abuse, right?

And so he asserts facts that counter any claim

of application of the discovery rule.

So it is a much earlier stage

than application of the discovery rule

would normally be applied, right?

As this court said in Troy,

that's usually a fact question.

And I do want to talk a little bit about some of the questions

that were asked about how this case is different than Troy.

So in Troy, we had a plaintiff, a student at a school

who was sexually assaulted by a teacher.

And she came forward and said

that she lacked knowledge that the school was aware

prior to the hiring of the teacher

that he had prior instances of sexual assault, right?

So this court said so there's a question of fact

that has to be answered as to inquiry notice.

There's a question of whether she knew

or reasonably could have discovered

the causal connection between her injuries,

the sexual assault that occurred in secret,

and the school's negligence

in hiring a teacher they knew

to have a history of sexual assault.

Here, there's no such question of fact

as to what the plaintiff knew

or and reasonably diligence could have discovered

because the plaintiff doesn't allege

that there are any facts about mom prospect

that he knows now that he didn't know

within the statute of limitations period.

There's no facts about mom prospects.

Actions, it's management, it's hiring

that the plaintiff alleges he knows now

and can plead as well-plied facts in his complaint

during the statute of limitations period.

What he says is I didn't know it was abusive, right?

I didn't know it was wrongful.

I didn't know I could hold somebody accountable

for that kind of action.

But that's exactly what this court said

and Sullyville is not enough.

Now, Sullyville is an unpublished decision,

but it's relatively recent and it is directly on point.

This court wrote that the discovery rule

does not toll the statute of limitations

until a plaintiff discerns the legal theory

by which his or her case can proceed.

Rather, it tolls the statute of limitations

only when the injury or its causal relationship

to the defendant's acts remains unknown to the plaintiff.

In that case, it had to do with a campground

and the operation of a campground, right?

And whether or not that operation of the campground

was in violation of the statute of limitations

or whether or not that operation of the campground

was in violation of the zoning rules, right?

And the court and the plaintiffs in that case

tried to say that the statute should be told

because I didn't know they weren't allowed

to run the campground.

And this court said no, you knew the campground was running,

you knew it was there, you knew it was causing injury

to your land or to your experience of your land

and therefore the discovery rule does not apply.

It does not.

So Troy, again, just says that just because you were assaulted

by somebody who was employed by a school or a facility, right?

And that assault happened on campus, like, you know,

at the business, that that alone is not like

a per se rule that the discovery rule can't apply.

That it's a fact question whether or not

there was information that you didn't have

that you couldn't bring your complaint, right?

And so Troy doesn't say that the discovery rule

applies in that case, right?

To make it timely.

Troy just says, as a matter of law,

we can't say that it doesn't.

And here the lower court considered that.

Said I can't just per se say that it's true.

But here, unlike Troy where she says

I didn't know the school knew about the prior sexual assault.

I didn't even know there was a prior sexual assault,

but I certainly didn't know that the school knew about it

when they chose to hire this teacher.

There's no fact about what mom presbyte did or didn't.

I see that my red light is on.

And so I think that that without the allegations

about fact, that that's what makes this different from Troy.

So for all those reasons and the reasons in our brief,

I'd ask you to affirm.

Thank you.

Thank you, Judge.

Justice Conway, just the back to your point about,

he complained to the police when they picked him up

when he went AWOL.

A very similar thing happened in Troy.

Larissa Troy was abused and within days,

actually went to the dean of students at Bishop Gertin

who said, oh, I don't think that happened

and you're going to get in trouble if you keep saying that.

You know, it's really very similar,

very similar fact.

Now she was 17.

13.

That's a relevant fact too.

Again, we're on a motion to dismiss.

The record isn't developed.

My recollection in Troy was on the motion for summary judgment.

Correct, it was.

They had a full record, even there,

the court construed the inferences improperly

against the plaintiff instead of in favor of.

To get to the pervasive of these assaults,

as my colleague Mary Trick just mentioned,

his interpretation is different

and you'll read it in his statement.

He says that to him,

because of the violent restraints

and the general abusiveness,

he didn't interpret it as abusiveness.

First he was told by them it wasn't abusive,

that this is what happens to kids like you

and we can do it, this is allowed.

But also because it was common,

he drew the very reasonable conclusion

that it's okay.

All the adults here are doing it.

If all the adults are doing it,

it must be okay.

That is a very reasonable inference,

a reasonable belief and he did believe that.

It goes to his knowledge about MPA.

He, you know,

if he believes that it's legitimate

and everybody's doing it,

that's a reasonable inference.

That's got to be construed in favor of him.

Instead, the judge here just ignored it.

I also want to point out that

he doesn't allege any facts that are different

between the time he's 13 and the time he's 27

when he actually found out about this.

An awful lot was different.

First, he grew up.

And second, you know, his brain finished forming.

And we submitted expert reports

on childhood development and childhood trauma

and the effect on kids like John Doe 533,

the court below ignored them.

Ignored them.

In fact, it should have construed them in our favor,

but did not.

There's a long list of things the court did not

construe in our favor.

I mentioned earlier about the intimacy of Mount Prospect.

I consider that to be the biggest one.

But that's just the first on a long list.

But the things that were also different

was at the age of 27 or 28,

he actually met with a pastor.

And the pastor helped him to understand

that now you're an adult, what happened to you back then

actually was abuse.

That wasn't legitimate. It wasn't permitted.

It wasn't lawful.

And yes, it was painful.

And just because it's painful doesn't mean it was abusive.

But, oh, I'm sorry you're on it.

There were a lot of difference between 13

and 27 or 28 when he filed.

Very similar actually to the Troy situation

where at the age of

over 40,

she started googling

sexual abuse and her athletic

history at Bishop Gerton and found this

inadvertently.

Thank you.

segments.text: This is case 2025-0092, John Doe number 533 versus New Hampshire Department of Health and Human Services at all.
Attorney Vincenzo, I understand you reserved two minutes for rebuttal.
Correct, Your Honor. Thank you.
May it please the Court. David Vincenzo for John Doe 533.
Your Honors, this case requires the Court to review the Superior Court's application of the discovery rule.
The Superior Court made improper, subjective assumptions about certain facts and resolved conflicting inferences against John Doe 533.
In doing so, it violated this Court's rule most recently articulated in a child abuse case in Troy versus Bishop Gerton, which is just positive here.
The language of the discovery rule is familiar to us all and it's briefed very adequately, so I won't repeat it.
But the standard enunciated in Troy or repeated in Troy is that all evidence and inferences drawn therefrom must be viewed in the light most favorable to the non-moving party.
Here, that's John Doe, the 13-year-old boy who was abused.
Here, as in Troy, the lower court misapplied that standard.
The Court made factual assumptions, not supported by the record evidence.
Of course, it's on a motion to dismiss, so there's hardly any record evidence.
The Court also construed disputed inferences against the plaintiff rather than in the plaintiff's favor.
And ignored other reasonable inferences favoring the plaintiff.
In fact, the Court ignored an awful lot of what the plaintiff said in his written declaration.
I'd really ask the Court to review it if you haven't done it already.
It said appendix one, pages 158 to 168.
There's so much there that the lower court just seemed not to pick up on.
All of this violated the rule on motion to dismiss and made all the difference.
The Court's review here is de novo.
We asked the Court to reverse and to send it back to discovery and ultimately summary judgment or a trial.
I want to go directly to what I consider the biggest error that the Court made,
because it's the most important, it appears to be really the linchpin on which the Court decided the case.
The Court hypothesized that Mount Prospect Academy was a small single group home and had that mental image,
which in its view, given its intimate character meant that the management of Mount Prospect
was physically adjacent or mingled with a resonance and therefore would know what was going on.
None of this is true.
Again, we don't have much of a record, but there is nothing in the record that suggests that MPA is a small intimate little facility.
It's irrelevant with respect to statute limitations, isn't it?
No, it's not, Your Honor, because it's the linchpin on which the Court made the decision on the discovery rule.
The Court theorized that because it's a small little intimate home.
Therefore, the management would be intermingled with the events, would know that the violence was going on,
and if it knew that it was going on and didn't stop it, it must be condoning it.
Then it drew another inference on top of that inference, which was based on a false assumption.
And that is that this 13-year-old boy should have seen all that and noticed that management is nearby,
they don't seem to be stopping it, therefore they must have known.
And therefore, he doesn't get the benefit of the discovery rule.
That's the basis of this decision.
That's in opposite of Troy.
Sorry, Your Honor?
That's in opposite of our holding in Troy, isn't it?
I think it is, Your Honor.
Exactly.
I think Troy controls this case.
I don't think the Court has a very difficult job here.
Regrettably, the lower Court, for whatever reason, somehow I had the idea that this is a small intimate home.
Mount Prospect Academy is actually bigger than the Sumeen Center.
